title: On Uniform Boundedness of Torsion Points for Abelian Varieties Over Function Fields
speaker: Jit Wu Yap
abstract: Let K be the function field of a smooth projective curve B over the complex numbers and let g be a positive integer. The uniform boundedness conjecture predicts that there exists a constant N, depending only on g and K, such that for any g-dimensional abelian variety A over K, any K-rational torsion point of A must have order at most N. In this talk, we will discuss some recent progress under the assumption that A has semistable reduction over K. This is joint work with Nicole Looper.

proof_stages.label: Semistable function-field setup and target theorems
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes K=C(B), defines semistable abelian varieties via semiabelian models, imposes absence of isotrivial subabelian varieties, and states uniform boundedness of rational torsion together with a Lang-Silverman lower bound for non-torsion generic points.

proof_stages.label: Height versus bad-reduction input and Szpiro analogue
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses the Faltings-Zhang/Vojta inequality bounding stable Faltings height by the number of bad places over function fields, and the analogous higher-dimensional Szpiro conjecture would provide the same input for number fields.

proof_stages.label: Tension between rationality constraints and equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: The guiding heuristic is that K-rationality plus the numerical inequality forces many reductions of the points into restricted geometric regions, whereas torsion or small-height points should equidistribute, creating a quantitative contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Transfinite-diameter replacement for local height energy
proof_stages.summary: A higher-dimensional transfinite-diameter/Green-energy function is introduced, later formalized via Looper's construction, to replace elliptic pairwise local heights and to detect when many points are nearly constrained by low-degree hypersurfaces.

proof_stages.label: Rescaled ultrafilter degeneration mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The speaker previews a compactness device in which a sequence of abelian varieties over valued fields is rescaled according to its distance from the moduli boundary and then passed to an ultrafilter limit that remains an abelian variety over a large valued field.

proof_stages.label: Elliptic-curve prototype of the contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Following Silverman's elliptic-curve proof, many mildly bad places are selected, a bounded multiple makes the chosen rational points reduce to the identity component there, and splitting the global sum of local Neron functions yields a positive contribution contradicting torsion or sufficiently small height when enough points are present.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-abelian-variety positivity from skeleton equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed abelian variety, Gubler's skeleton and a Bloch-Eriksson equidistribution theorem show that if the Looper energy tends to zero then retractions equidistribute on the real torus, so points confined to the identity-component region at a bad place must instead have a positive local energy; the everywhere-good-reduction case is handled separately by bounded Faltings height.

proof_stages.label: Uniformity via ultrafilter limits
proof_stages.summary: To make the positive lower bound uniform in families, one assumes failure of uniformity, takes a worsening sequence, forms the rescaled ultrafilter limit in the fine moduli space, and applies the pointwise valued-field positivity theorem to contradict the limiting configuration.

examples.name: Positive-characteristic failure via supersingular varieties
examples.note: In response to a question about characteristic p, the speaker notes that supersingular abelian varieties make the analogous uniform boundedness statement false, clarifying the characteristic-zero scope of the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only states that positive-characteristic uniform boundedness is false for supersingular varieties, without constructing or analyzing such a variety

passes.reason: Supersingular abelian varieties are an extremal Newton-polygon/p-rank-zero locus and are cited as making the theorem false in positive characteristic.
Supersingular abelian varieties have the extreme all-slope-1/2 Newton polygon and are cited as making the positive-characteristic statement false.
Supersingular varieties have extremal positive-characteristic Newton/p-rank behavior and are cited as making the theorem false in characteristic p.
Supersingular abelian varieties have extremal positive-characteristic Newton/p-torsion behavior and are invoked because uniform boundedness is false there.
Supersingular abelian varieties form an extremal positive-characteristic Newton-polygon/p-rank locus and are cited to show the theorem fails in characteristic p.
Supersingular abelian varieties have the extremal all-slope-1/2/minimal-p-rank Newton-polygon structure and are cited to make the positive-characteristic boundedness statement false.
Supersingular abelian varieties are used to show the positive-characteristic UBC statement is false and are extremal in their Newton/p-rank behavior.
Supersingular abelian varieties are used to show the positive-characteristic analogue fails, and they form an extremal Newton/p-rank stratum rather than a sporadic exception.
Supersingular varieties are cited as the positive-characteristic cases where uniform boundedness fails, and supersingularity is an extreme Newton-slope/p-rank condition.

supersingular positive-characteristic abelian varieties are invoked to show the characteristic-zero uniform boundedness statement fails, and they have extremal p-divisible/Newton behavior rather than being sporadic exceptions.

examples.name: Six points in the plane and closeness to a conic
examples.note: The classical fact that five general points in P^2 determine a conic, while six points may be only nearly on one, is used to make the transfinite-diameter quantity concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: six points near a conic are used as an illustration of what the transfinite diameter measures, without evaluating or working through particular points

passes.reason: Six plane points are the critical first overdetermined number for testing whether points lie close to a conic.

examples.name: Rational map Green function and the resultant
examples.note: As a simple requested illustration of the ultrafilter compactness principle, the speaker describes bounding a pairwise Green function in terms of the resultant of a rational map by degenerating to a good-reduction limit.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the ultrafilter degeneration idea on a rational-map Green-function/resultant example to explain how a contradiction gives the desired bound
